333
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission
to photocopy this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details).
Distress Tolerance Handout 7   (Distress Tolerance Worksheets 5‚Äì5b)
Distracting
A way to remember these skills is the phrase ‚ÄúWise Mind ACCEPTS.‚Äù
With Activities:

¬â Focus attention on a task you need to get
done.

¬â Rent movies; watch TV.

¬â Clean a room in your house.

¬â Find an event to go to.

¬â Play computer games.

¬â Go walking. Exercise.

¬â Surf the Internet. Write e-mails.

¬â Play sports.

¬â Go out for a meal or eat a favorite food.

¬â Call or go out with a friend.

¬â Listen to your iPod; download music.

¬â Build something.

¬â Spend time with your children.

¬â Play cards.

¬â Read magazines, books, comics.

¬â Do crossword puzzles or Sudoku.

¬â Other:
With Contributing:

¬â Find volunteer work to do.

¬â Help a friend or family member.

¬â Surprise someone with something nice (a
card, a favor, a hug).

¬â Give away things you don‚Äôt need.

¬â Call or send an instant message encouraging
someone or just saying hi.

¬â Make something nice for someone else.

¬â Do something thoughtful.

¬â Other:
With Comparisons:

¬â Compare how you are feeling now to a time
when you felt different.

¬â Think about people coping the same as you or
less well than you.

¬â Compare yourself to those less fortunate.

¬â Watch reality shows about others‚Äô troubles;
read about disasters, others‚Äô suffering.

¬â Other:
With different Emotions:

¬â Read emotional books or stories, old letters.

¬â Watch emotional TV shows; go to emotional
movies.

¬â Listen to emotional music.
(Be sure the event creates different emotions.)
Ideas: Scary movies, joke books, comedies,
funny records, religious music, soothing music
or music that fires you up, going to a store and
reading funny greeting cards.

¬â Other:
With Pushing away:

¬â Push the situation away by leaving it for a
while.

¬â Leave the situation mentally.

¬â Build an imaginary wall between yourself and
the situation.

¬â Block thoughts and images from your mind.

¬â Notice ruminating: Yell ‚ÄúNo!‚Äù

¬â Refuse to think about the painful situations.

¬â Put the pain on a shelf. Box it up and put it
away for a while.

¬â Deny the problem for the moment.

¬â Other:
With other Thoughts:

¬â Count to 10; count colors in a painting or
poster or out the window; count anything.

¬â Repeat words to a song in your mind.

¬â Work puzzles.

¬â Watch TV or read.

¬â Other:
With other Sensations:

¬â Squeeze a rubber ball very hard.

¬â Listen to very loud music.

¬â Hold ice in your hand or mouth.

¬â Go out in the rain or snow.

¬â Take a hot or cold shower.

¬â Other:

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: Distracting A way to remember these skills is the phrase ‚ÄúWise Mind ACCEPTS.‚Äù With Activities: 
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Distress tolerance means getting through the wave without adding harm. Use gentle, medically safer versions first.
tiny_steps: Choose one body-safe skill for 30 seconds.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Replace intense exercise with hand squeeze, paced exhale, safe fidget, weighted comfort if tolerated, or visual grounding.
Avoid cold shock/breath-holding/intense exercise unless already clinician-approved and safe that day.
Keep oxygen, rescue meds, allergy plan, mobility aids, and exit routes accessible.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not use crisis skills as punishment, sensory flooding, or exposure without consent.
